text: abandonment, but quickly shook the notion away. She knew Gloria didn’t know of her struggle, and the barista wasn’t responsible for Erin’s healing. Erin considered heading down to the first floor and using the front door. The ground floor boasted a spacious patio area in the center, surrounded by several small food stands and an all-night copy center. It was well lit and likely occupied. She wouldn’t be alone. But she would still need to walk around to the parking garage and go up to the second level. She would need to walk all the way to the back wall where she had bravely followed Gloria’s parking lead that morning. I’m a grown woman. I cannot stand here and cower all night. She hissed rather than exhaled, determination finding its way past her worry. Erin dug into her shoulder bag and retrieved her keys, gave the elevators and dark coffee nook one more glance, and then turned toward the door. Erin took several quick strides to reach the exterior door and pushed it with more force than necessary, as if she were proving her willpower and inner strength with the show of exaggerated confidence. Eyes forward, she hurried through the skywalk without looking at the street below or the darkening sky above. She burst through the metal door with vigor but froze in the night air beyond. The garage, mottled gray with bright repair patches on the walls and dark vehicle fluid stains on the ground, was industrialized tie-dye. And it was empty. A handful of parked cars dotted the open expanse, but no other living soul occupied the level. The overhead lights buzzed and Erin’s shoulders tightened, as if the sound had physically touched her. Erin couldn’t see her car behind the elevator block, which was positioned in the center of the garage. But she knew exactly where she’d parked and headed toward it. Barely a dozen yards in and her mouth went dry. The small sounds of the garage were tiny punctuation marks to her dark thoughts. Erin imagined the rustle of paper was the casual shuffling of a perpetrator’s quiet shoes. The flicker of the lights moved shadows and gave them life, breath. She stopped and looked around. The doctor’s wisdom crawled into her mind and Erin whispered to the quiet garage, “Assess the truth.” The first time she had heard the phrase, Erin had been in the midst of a full-blown breakdown, slumped to the floor of the doctor’s office, knees pulled tight to her chest, crying with such anguish as she tried to discuss the rape. “Assess the truth.” The therapist had joined Erin on the floor and gently held her shoulders. “You need to calm down and remember. Don’t let your brain panic and fill in details that aren’t true. Don’t presume or conflate. Examine what really happened.” “But I remember it. He pushed up my skirt and—.” “No. No, he did not.” Doctor Mason had paused for what felt like hours, watching Erin. Erin shook her head, and the doctor finally pushed the truth forward for Erin. “You were attacked. You were mugged. But you were not sexually assaulted. I’ve read the file. You’ve read the file. You weren’t even wearing a skirt that day. You were—” “I had on my blue pantsuit. I hated that pantsuit.” The details cleared, and as Erin sat on the expensive rug in the doctor’s office, she started to remember the actual attack. It had been violent, but not a violation. It had been traumatic, but it had been so on its own merit, and Erin didn’t need to make it any worse or deepen the stains it would already leave. Her life had been threatened, but not her honor. Erin looked around again and assessed her reality, grounding herself in logic. Business hours were over, so the garage was empty. The lights always flickered—this was neither new nor foreboding. The shadows were just that—shadows. And her car was up ahead, albeit behind the elevator bank. I was attacked, but I survived. Hell, I survived therapy. I can survive an empty garage. Erin continued, her pace still slow and cautious, but steady. After several moments, Erin realized she was flinching with each step, not from trepidation but as a reaction to her own movements. The sharp echoes of her high heels were like a constant tap of anticipation. The slow dripping of a leaking sink keeping you awake at night. The ticking of a clock your insomnia hugs for company. Erin reached down and removed her heels, carrying them together in her left hand, her right already defensively gripping her keys, the metal protruding between her fingers. With her steps silent, her thoughts could scream and echo in the cavity of residual pain where her fears remained alive. Erin hadn’t been raped, but she had been assaulted. His initial attack had been as he stepped from between the cars and simply appeared in front of Erin, pushing her unexpectedly. Erin had lost her footing and went down, hard, hitting her tailbone on the unforgiving cement floor of the garage. He yanked her purse from her grip, and when Erin reached up reflexively to stop him, he grabbed her arm and twisted it, forcing her to spin around. He pushed, leveraging her shoulder into the cold concrete, and lowered himself, straddling Erin. He leaned in and whispered, the whiskey fumes poisoning the air between them. “Stay down. If you move, if you scream, I will kill you and put you in your own trunk and they won’t find you for days.” He licked her ear, and Erin whimpered. Her saliva-moistened flesh chilled in the night air, as Erin’s mouth became hot and bile threatened the back of her throat. She squeezed her eyes shut, her lip quivering, tears building behind her lids. “Do you understand?” His raspy words filled her mind with images Erin would dream about for months. “You’ll bloat.
